query: treatment of choice in childhood thyrotoxicosis carbimazole vs methimazole

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- Treatment / Management -- Radioiodine Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Children with thyrotoxicosis may be treated with methimazole, radioiodine therapy, or thyroidectomy. Methimazole is the first-line therapy for Graves disease in children, typically administered for 1 to 2 years, as some children may achieve remission. Radioiodine therapy is not recommended for children aged 5 or younger. Additionally, PTU should be avoided in children due to its risk of hepatotoxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Methimazole -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, alternative antithyroid therapies, such as propylthiouracil or surgical intervention, are the preferred choices for healthcare professionals during the first trimester. Physicians might recommend switching to methimazole during the second and third trimesters of pregnancy due to the risk of hepatotoxicity associated with propylthiouracil. The lowest effective dose of methimazole should be used to maintain a euthyroid state during pregnancy to prevent fetal goiter and cretinism. Thyroid function should be closely monitored, with regular weekly or biweekly assessments, during pregnancy and the postpartum periods to enable dosage adjustments for methimazole as needed. Women of childbearing age should discuss contraception with their healthcare providers before initiating methimazole therapy. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Methimazole -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric patients : Methimazole is prescribed to treat hyperthyroidism in patients unsuitable for surgery or radioactive iodine therapy. The initial dosage of methimazole for pediatric patients is typically 0.5 to 0.7 mg/kg/d, divided into 3 doses and administered approximately every 8 hours. The maintenance dosage typically amounts to 0.2 mg/kg/d, which is roughly half the initial dose. Methimazole doses should be adjusted according to the serum TSH level and clinical response of patients. Maintenance doses rarely exceed 30 mg/d when taken orally or 1 mg/kg/d in cases of severe hyperthyroidism. The safety and efficacy of methimazole in pediatric patients younger than 1 have not been established. Prolonged or high-dose use of methimazole in children may lead to growth retardation. Therefore, it is essential to regularly monitor the growth and development of pediatric patients undergoing methimazole therapy. Methimazole may also cause severe adverse effects in children, including agranulocytosis, hepatotoxicity, vasculitis, and congenital malformations. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Methimazole -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Methimazole has been studied extensively in numerous clinical trials to assess its efficacy and safety. A randomized clinical trial reported that continuous methimazole therapy for 5 years led to an 84% remission rate, which persisted for up to 4 years after drug withdrawal in patients with Graves disease. [3] A parallel study compared the thyroid status of individuals who discontinued methimazole treatment after 12.8 years with those who continued methimazole for 24 years in patients with Graves disease or toxic multinodular goiter. The results showed that long-term methimazole treatment was effective and safe for preventing relapse and maintaining euthyroidism in patients. [4] An additional study assessed the safety and effectiveness of long-term methimazole treatment for toxic nodular goiters in patients unsuitable for surgery or radioactive iodine therapy. The study found that methimazole therapy effectively restored normal thyroid function and reduced goiter size in most patients with relatively minimal adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Methimazole -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients with hyperthyroidism suitable for methimazole therapy based on clinical presentation, thyroid function tests, and medical history. Assess the efficacy of methimazole treatment by regularly monitoring thyroid function tests, clinical symptoms, and adverse effects. Implement methimazole therapy in accordance with established guidelines and dosing protocols, considering patient-specific factors such as age and comorbidities. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare teams to develop comprehensive, patient-centered treatment plans and ensure coordinated care to treat hyperthyroidism and improve patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism -- Treatment / Management -- ATDs
retrieved_docs.content: 10 mg of carbimazole is converted to approximately 6 mg of methimazole [1] An equivalent dosage ratio of propylthiouracil to methimazole is 20:1. This ratio is recommended for dose conversions when switching between these agents. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Toxic Goiter -- Treatment / Management -- Antithyroidthyroid Drugs (ATD)
retrieved_docs.content: The American Thyroid Association and American Association of Clinical Endocrinology recommend methimazole as the preferred drug for Graves disease, except in patients with adverse reactions to the drug or women in the first trimester of pregnancy. It is preferred over propylthiouracil due to better efficacy, longer half-life and duration of action, and ease of dosing once a day.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism -- Treatment / Management -- ATDs
retrieved_docs.content: Attaining a euthyroid status typically requires several months after initiation of thionamide therapy. Although methimazole and PTU are equally effective, methimazole is preferred due to once-daily dosing and a relatively better safety profile. An exception to this recommendation is in pregnant patients, in which PTU is preferred. Methimazole is associated with an increased risk of congenital defects, and thus, PTU is preferred in managing hyperthyroidism during pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Methimazole -- Indications -- Off-Label Use
retrieved_docs.content: Methimazole is also used off-label for treating thyrotoxicosis or thyroid storm. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Most nonpregnant women with Graves' disease use methimazole due to a lower risk of hepatotoxicity than PTU. [9] Some women may prefer to switch to PTU before becoming pregnant. If a patient chooses to remain on methimazole until after becoming pregnant, it is important to counsel the patient on the risk of methimazole embryopathy if she is not changed to PTU before organogenesis. [3] [5] [8] [14] When compared to the risk of methimazole embryopathy, the risk of PTU-induced hepatotoxicity is lower. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- Treatment / Management -- Thionamide Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Methimazole is administered at a dosage of 15 to 30 mg/d for 4 to 8 weeks in the treatment of Graves disease, during which most patients achieve a euthyroid state. Once euthyroidism is established, treatment can proceed with 1 of 2 approaches. In the block-replace method, the same dose of thionamide is continued to inhibit thyroid hormone production, while levothyroxine is added to maintain euthyroidism. Alternatively, the thionamide dose can be gradually reduced to allow endogenous thyroid hormone synthesis, thereby sustaining a euthyroid state. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Methimazole ," for more information. In Graves disease, long-term remission is achieved in about 50% of patients treated with thionamide drugs. However, a drawback of thionamides is the uncertainty of relapse after discontinuation. Prolonged treatment beyond 18 months has not been shown to improve remission rates. Methimazole is more effective and has a longer half-life than PTU, allowing for once-daily dosing.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism -- Treatment / Management -- ATDs
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy: Propylthiouracil is the preferred drug in the first trimester, associated with lower incidence and severity of embryopathy than methimazole. [58] [59] The treatment can be switched to methimazole after 16 weeks of gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management -- Antithyroid Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: After the first trimester, when most organogenesis is complete, patients are then transitioned to methimazole. This transition is necessary to decrease the likelihood of hepatotoxicity, which is a rare but severe adverse event associated with PTU. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Toxic Goiter -- Treatment / Management -- Antithyroidthyroid Drugs (ATD)
retrieved_docs.content: The antithyroid drug options are propylthiouracil, methimazole, and carbimazole.

retrieved_docs.title: Propylthiouracil (PTU) -- Administration -- Propylthiouracil and Patient Population Groups
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnant: PTU is a pregnancy category D drug. PTU can cross the placenta and can cause fetal cretinism and goiter. Methimazole causes fetal anatomical abnormalities; hence, if it is necessary to use antithyroid drugs in pregnancy, PTU is preferred in the first trimester with the lowest possible drug dose usage. Due to the increased reported risk of maternal hepatotoxicity from PTU, methimazole is the therapeutic choice in the second and third trimesters. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Graves Disease -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: All three options have pros and cons, and there is no consensus on which one is the best option. It is very important to discuss all three options in detail with the patients and make an individualized decision.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 500
retrieved_docs.content: THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS is a therapeutic group that encompasses dextrothyroxine as one of its child agents, while it is itself classified under the broader parent category of antithyroid preparations. Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents used to manage conditions of excess thyroid hormone production (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ, i.e., hyperthyroidism). These preparations are chemically related to diiodotyrosine, indicating that they belong to the diiodotyrosine class of compounds, and they also include specific drugs such as carbimazole (karbimazol). Together, these relationships illustrate how dextrothyroxine, antithyroid preparations, diiodotyrosine, and carbimazole are interconnected within the management of thyroid disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1443
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are a class of medications that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis, and they encompass specific agents such as diiodotyrosine and carbimazole. Both diiodotyrosine and carbimazole function as antithyroid drugs, sharing the common therapeutic purpose of reducing excessive thyroid hormone production in conditions like hyperthyroidism. Recognizing that these compounds belong to the broader category of antithyroid preparations helps clinicians select appropriate treatment options and understand their pharmacologic relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2201
retrieved_docs.content: Karbimazol, also known as carbimazole, is classified as an antithyroid preparation and is used in the treatment of hyperthyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2751
retrieved_docs.content: THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS include dextrothyroxin as a specific child preparation, indicating that dextrothyroxin is a type of medication within the broader category of thyroid and antithyroid agents used to influence thyroid hormone activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2174
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid extracts and thyroid inhibitors represent a therapeutic class of agents that affect thyroid function, and within this class the specific subgroup known as thyreostatika is included. Thyreostatika are medications that act to modulate or suppress thyroid activity, and they are classified as a child category of the broader group of thyroid extracts and thyroid inhibitors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate descriptor in the clinical management of تمزق, thyroid disorders, بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, and ورم عضلي أملس, indicating that it may be used when documenting or planning treatment for each of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents that counteract excess thyroid hormone production and are employed in the treatment of hyperthyroidism (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ). By inhibiting the synthesis of thyroid hormones, these medications help normalize elevated metabolic activity, alleviate symptoms such as tachycardia, weight loss, and tremor, and reduce the risk of complications associated with uncontrolled hyperthyroidism. Effective use of antithyroid preparations is therefore a central component of managing patients with فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 720
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified as a subset of the broader category of medications and vaccines. Within this therapeutic class, specific chemical agents such as carbimazole and diiodotyrosine are recognized as types of antithyroid preparations, reflecting the diversity of compounds used to inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis. The hierarchy further places hormone antagonists under antithyroid preparations, with PD 134308 identified as an example of a hormone‑antagonist agent. The grouping “THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS” is itself a child category of antithyroid preparations, and it includes dextrothyroxin as one of its constituent drugs. This organized structure clarifies how various thyroid‑targeting agents relate to one another and to the larger pharmacologic domain of medications, aiding clinicians in selecting and understanding treatments that modulate thyroid function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2074
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified as a type of diiodotyrosine, a compound that participates in thyroid hormone synthesis, and they are employed to inhibit thyroid hormone production in the management of hyperthyroid conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 380
retrieved_docs.content: THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS is positioned within the broader class of antithyroid preparations, indicating that it represents a subcategory of drugs that modulate thyroid activity. Within THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS, dextrothyroxin is identified as a specific child agent, reflecting its role as a distinct medication in this therapeutic group. Antithyroid preparations themselves encompass individual compounds such as dijodtyrosin and karbimazol, both of which are recognized as specific types of antithyroid agents. This hierarchical organization highlights how antithyroid preparations serve as the overarching category for various thyroid‑targeting drugs, with THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS and its child dextrothyroxin representing more specialized subsets, and dijodtyrosin and karbimazol exemplifying concrete pharmacologic options used in the management of hyperthyroid conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified within the broader category of medications and vaccines, reflecting their role as therapeutic agents used to manage thyroid disorders. These preparations encompass specific compounds such as karbimazol and dijodtyrosin, which are individual antithyroid drugs that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis. By being part of the medication and vaccine group, antithyroid preparations are recognized for their clinical importance in treating conditions like hyperthyroidism, and their inclusion of agents such as karbimazol and dijodtyrosin highlights the diversity of pharmacologic options available within this therapeutic class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1560
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations, exemplified by the drug karbimazol (carbimazole), function by interfering with the synthesis of diiodotyrosine, a crucial intermediate in thyroid hormone production. In clinical use, these antithyroid agents are associated with the condition described as فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (excess of blood or hyperemia), which may appear as a physiological response or side effect. Recognizing that karbimazol belongs to the broader class of antithyroid preparations and that its mechanism involves diiodotyrosine helps clinicians effectively treat hyperthyroid disorders while monitoring for signs of hyperemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2175
retrieved_docs.content: Triiodothyronine (trijodthyronin) is a thyroid hormone that is synthesized and secreted by the thyroid gland (štítná žláza). The štítná žláza is also the primary anatomical site where thyroid insufficiency (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة), a condition characterized by reduced production of thyroid hormones, is identified. Recognizing the relationship between trijodthyronin and the štítná žláza, as well as the gland’s role in قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة, is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of disorders affecting thyroid hormone metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: agents in the subcategory “Thyroid Extracts and Thyroid Inhibitors.” This subcategory contains the child entity thyreostatika (thyroid‑suppressing agents) used in hyperthyroidism. The thyroid gland is the anatomical source and site of action for these hormones and for thyroid‑blocking agents, and it is identified as the primary finding site for hypothyroidism (Arabic: قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة). Together, these interconnected classifications provide clinicians, pharmacists, and health‑information systems with a structured framework to locate and relate drugs, anatomical terms, and disease entities, supporting accurate prescribing, comprehensive patient care, and effective disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid Extracts and Thyroid Inhibitors comprise a category that includes the subclass thyreostatika, which are agents specifically related to the function of the štítná žláza (thyroid gland). Both thyreostatika and the thyroid hormone trijodthyronin (triiodothyronine) are directly associated with the štítná žláza, reflecting their roles in modulating thyroid activity. The štítná žláza is also identified as the anatomical finding site for قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة (hypothyroidism), underscoring the clinical relevance of these substances and the gland in the diagnosis and management of thyroid disorders.
